nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef environments including lagoons, protected reef slopes, and upper reef zones with moderate to high water flow

decorPreferences: exposed ledge or rock face for mounting

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Has been documented as an aggressive feeder that will accept supplemental target feedings of mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, zooplankton, and other small meaty foods. Amino acids and phytoplankton have been reported to enhance coloration and growth. Weekly or bi-weekly target feeding is generally recommended but not mandatory.

goodWith: corals placed well out of reach (15+ cm clearance)
avoidWith: adjacent LPS corals without adequate spacing
adjacent SPS corals without adequate spacing
soft corals placed within sweeper range

summary: Hydnophora is a genus of stony corals in Merulinidae with branching and encrusting morphs. Classified as LPS/SPS-like, it is one of the more aggressive corals in the hobby; sweeper tentacles and mesenterial filaments can damage neighboring corals, requiring substantial spacing.
careNotes:
Hydnophora is a genus (~6 species) commonly traded as Hydnophora sp. in the aquarium hobby, with H. exesa (encrusting) and H. rigida (branching/velvet horn) being the most frequently encountered. The scientificName is set to 'Hydnophora sp.' to reflect this trade-level convention.

Classification note: Hydnophora straddles the LPS/SPS boundary in the hobby — it has a calcified skeleton like SPS corals but polyp anatomy and behavior more similar to LPS. It is housed in family Merulinidae alongside SPS-like genera, but most retailers list it as LPS or 'SPS-like LPS'. The category field reflects 'LPS' consistent with common trade classification.

Aggression — this coral is considered among the more aggressive stony corals in the aquarium trade:
• After lights out, Hydnophora has been known to extend sweeper tentacles reaching approximately 5-8 cm (2-3 inches) that can sting and damage neighboring corals.
• Even without extended tentacles, Hydnophora has been documented extruding mesenterial filaments with digestive enzymes when neighboring corals come within 2-4 cm, causing significant tissue damage or death to the adjacent coral.
• A minimum clearance of 15-20 cm (6-8 inches) from all neighboring corals is generally advised.
• Growth rate is reported as relatively fast; colonies should be monitored and fragged to prevent them from encroaching on neighbors.

Lighting: Generally advised to acclimate new specimens gradually, starting in moderate PAR (150 PAR) and slowly moving up to target zone (200-300 PAR). Color fades in insufficient light. Cross-source PAR data ranges from 75-350; 150-300 PAR covers the consensus moderate-to-high range.

Water chemistry: Like all stony corals, Hydnophora consumes calcium and alkalinity as it grows. Alkalinity swings should be avoided; consistent dosing or a calcium reactor is recommended in tanks with multiple stony corals. Nitrates 1-10 ppm and phosphate 0.01-0.1 ppm are generally acceptable.

Growth: Both branching (H. rigida) and encrusting (H. exesa) morphs are available. Branching morphs grow upward and outward; encrusting morphs spread laterally across rockwork. Either form can become a dominant colony in a well-maintained system.

Cross-source temperature disagreement: LiveAquaria lists 72-78°F (22-26°C) while Vivid Aquariums and TankFacts list 75-80°F (24-27°C). Averaged range 23-27°C adopted.

Alkalinity cross-source: LiveAquaria and AlgaeBarn list 8-12 dKH; BRS and Vivid list 8-9 dKH. Averaged upper bound ~11 dKH adopted.

CITES: Hydnophora sp. is listed under CITES Appendix II (export regulated). Aquaculture specimens are widely available from ORA, ECC, and other aquaculture facilities and are preferred.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the primary propagation method in captivity. Branches can be cut with bone cutters or a bandsaw and mounted on frag plugs; encrusting morphs can be chipped off the base rock. Aquaculture specimens (ORA, ECC brands) are widely available and acclimate more readily than wild-collected colonies.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Encrusting Horn Coral
primary.notes: Primary source for scientific name (Hydnophora exesa), care level (moderate), temperament (aggressive), water parameters (temperature 72-78°F, SG 1.023-1.025, pH 8.1-8.4, kH 8-12), feeding notes, and aggression description.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply – WWC Glowstick Hydnophora
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 75-78°F, SG 1.024-1.026, pH 8.1-8.4, kH 8-9 dKH, Ca 380-450 ppm, Mg 1300-1450 ppm. Lighting listed as 75-250 PAR; flow as moderate-to-high. Confirmed aggressive temperament and fast growth rate.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn – ORA Green Hydnophora
additional.notes: Cross-checked parameters: temp 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, salinity 32-35 ppt (≈SG 1.025-1.027), kH 8-12 dKH, Ca 350-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm. PAR ~250 cited. Confirmed high aggression (described as 'potent sting that kills any coral it touches') and feeding behavior.

additional.name: Vivid Aquariums – Green Hydnophora
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temp 75-80°F (24-27°C), SG 1.024-1.026, pH 8.1-8.4, kH 8-9.5, Ca 420-440, Mg 1260-1350. PAR 250-350. Confirmed 2-3 inch (5-8 cm) spacing requirement and aggressive temperament. Noted Indo-Pacific native range.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Hydnophora rigida
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Order Scleractinia, Family Merulinidae), native range (Indo-Pacific: Australia, Philippines, Fiji, Indonesia, Samoa, Solomon Islands, Madagascar), and depth range (1-30 m). Habitat: lagoons and protected reef slopes.
